one_liner: A 34-year-old male returning from Nigeria 10 days ago presents with cyclical high fevers, rigors, profuse sweating, headache, and jaundice; he did not take this condition prophylaxis.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient returned from a 3-week business trip to Lagos, Nigeria 10 days ago. He was advised to take antithis conditionl prophylaxis but did not fill the prescription. Five days ago, he developed sudden onset of high fevers (up to 40.5°C) with violent shaking chills lasting 1-2 hours, followed by drenching sweats and transient defervescence. The febrile episodes have been recurring every 24-48 hours. He reports severe headache, myalgias, nausea, two episodes of non-bloody vomiting, and progressive fatigue. He noticed yellowing of his eyes 2 days ago. He denies altered mental status, seizures, dark urine (other than concentrated), shortness of breath, or bleeding. No insect repellent use during travel.
stage2.family_history: Non-contributory. Parents are Nigerian-American; patient was born and raised in the US.
stage2.social_history: Software engineer. Born in the US. Frequent business travel to West Africa (2-3 trips per year). Non-smoker. Social alcohol. No illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Acutely ill-appearing male with visible rigors and diaphoresis. Scleral icterus present. No conjunctival pallor. Skin warm, jaundiced. No rash or petechiae. Abdomen soft, spleen palpable 4 cm below left costal margin, mildly tender. Liver edge palpable 2 cm below the right costal margin. No ascites. No lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam: alert and oriented, no focal deficits, no neck stiffness. No signs of respiratory distress.

lab_results.result: Thick smear: Plasmodium species identified. Thin smear: this condition trophozoites with characteristic 'headphone' double chromatin dots and multiple ring forms per RBC. Parasitemia: 4.8%.
lab_results.clinical_significance: confirms the suspected diagnosis this condition with significant parasitemia (>2% considered high for falciparum)

lab_results.result: Positive for this condition HRP-2 antigen
lab_results.clinical_significance: Supports this condition diagnosis; RDT complements but does not replace blood smear
Anemia from hemolysis of parasitized and unparasitized red blood cells
Thrombocytopenia is very common in this condition (present in >80% of falciparum cases)
Normal renal function; monitor closely as AKI is a complication of severe falciparum this condition
Normal glucose; hypoglycemia is a marker of severe this condition
Mild transaminitis common in this condition from hepatic sequestration of parasitized RBCs

imaging_results.result: No infiltrates, effusions, or consolidation. Heart size normal.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No evidence of pulmonary edema or ARDS, which can complicate severe falciparum this condition

key_tests: Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigens
low_yield_tests: Malaria serology/antibody testing (not useful for acute diagnosis)

stage5.final_clue: A traveler returning from West Africa without antithis conditionl prophylaxis presenting with cyclical fevers with rigors, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, and laboratory evidence of hemolytic anemia.

debrief.classic_presentation: A febrile traveler returning from a malaria-endemic area (Sub-Saharan Africa accounts for >90% of cases) presenting with cyclical fevers with rigors and sweating, headache, myalgias, and hepatosplenomegaly. P. falciparum malaria is the most dangerous species with highest parasitemia levels, and complications including cerebral malaria, severe anemia, ARDS, renal failure, and hypoglycemia. Thrombocytopenia is nearly universal. The classic lab triad is anemia + thrombocytopenia + elevated LDH with hemolytic features.
debrief.pathophysiology: P. falciparum sporozoites are injected by the female Anopheles mosquito into the bloodstream, travel to the liver, and undergo schizogony in hepatocytes (exoerythrocytic cycle, 5-7 days). Merozoites are released and invade red blood cells, undergoing asexual replication (erythrocytic cycle, ~48 hours for falciparum). RBC lysis releases merozoites and pyrogens, causing the cyclical fevers. P. falciparum is uniquely dangerous because: (1) it can infect RBCs of all ages (other species prefer reticulocytes or mature RBCs), leading to high parasitemia; (2) it induces expression of PfEMP1 on infected RBC surfaces, causing cytoadherence to vascular endothelium (sequestration), leading to microvascular obstruction in the brain (cerebral malaria), kidneys, and lungs; (3) the rosetting phenomenon (infected RBCs adhering to uninfected RBCs) further worsens microvascular obstruction.
debrief.treatment_overview: Uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (able to tolerate PO, parasitemia <5%, no end-organ damage): artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) — atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) 4 tablets daily for 3 days OR artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) for 3 days. Severe malaria (parasitemia ≥5%, altered mental status, severe anemia, ARDS, renal failure, hypoglycemia, acidosis): IV artesunate (first-line; available through CDC) followed by a complete oral ACT course. Exchange transfusion considered for parasitemia >10%. Monitor glucose Q4-6h (quinine/quinidine cause hypoglycemia). Repeat blood smears at 12, 24, 48, and 72 hours to document clearance. Admission for monitoring. Counsel on prophylaxis for future travel (atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine).
high_yield_pearls: Thrombocytopenia in a febrile returning traveler from Sub-Saharan Africa is malaria until proven otherwise.
P. falciparum can be rapidly fatal — always determine species and parasitemia urgently; parasitemia >5% or any end-organ dysfunction qualifies as severe malaria requiring IV artesunate.
A negative single blood smear does not exclude malaria — repeat thick and thin smears every 12-24 hours for at least 3 cycles if clinical suspicion remains high.
IV artesunate is the treatment of choice for severe malaria and is available in the US through the CDC.
Semi-immune individuals (those raised in endemic areas) may have lower parasitemia and milder symptoms; this patient, born in the US, lacks acquired immunity despite Nigerian heritage.
sources: CDC Yellow Book: Health Information for International Travel — Malaria (2024) — https://www.cdc.gov/guidelines/
UpToDate: Treatment of severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria in adults (2024) — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search
Lalloo DG et al. UK malaria treatment guidelines 2016. J Infect. 2016;72(6):635-649. — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/

after_stage2_more_likely: P. falciparum malaria — cyclical fevers with rigors, travel to West Africa without prophylaxis, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly; classic presentation
Typhoid fever — also presents with fever after Africa/Asia travel, but typically has relative bradycardia and stepwise fever rather than cyclical rigors
after_stage2_less_likely: Dengue fever — Nigeria is not a high-risk dengue area; dengue typically features more rash, retro-orbital pain, and severe myalgias ('breakbone fever')
Viral hepatitis — would not typically explain cyclical fevers with rigors and splenomegaly
after_labs_top3: P. falciparum malaria — confirmed by peripheral blood smear showing P. falciparum trophozoites at 4.8% parasitemia, positive RDT, hemolytic anemia, and thrombocytopenia
Typhoid fever — excluded by positive malaria smear; no leukopenia as typically seen in typhoid
Hemolytic anemia (other cause) — the hemolysis is explained by the confirmed malaria parasitemia
